questions.question: Which command sets an encrypted, MD5/type-5 privileged EXEC password?
questions.explanation: `enable secret` uses type 5 (MD5) and is always encrypted. It takes precedence over `enable password` when both are set.

questions.question: What is the difference between `enable password` and `enable secret`?
options: `enable password` is more secure than `enable secret`
`enable secret` is MD5/type-5 encrypted and takes precedence when both are set
They are identical — one is a shortcut for the other
`enable password` only works in user EXEC mode
questions.explanation: `enable password` stores cleartext (unless `service password-encryption` is on); `enable secret` is MD5-hashed and takes precedence if both are configured.

questions.question: What does `service password-encryption` do?
options: Encrypts current and future passwords using weak type-7 encryption
Forces all logins through a RADIUS server
Has no effect on `enable password` but encrypts `enable secret`
questions.explanation: `service password-encryption` applies weak type-7 (easily cracked) encryption to current and future passwords. It has no effect on `enable secret`, which is already MD5.

questions.question: In which mode does the prompt end with `#` and you can view everything plus save and restart the device?
questions.explanation: Privileged EXEC mode (entered via `enable`) shows a `#` prompt and gives complete view access plus restart and save.

questions.question: Which three commands are equivalent ways to save the running configuration?
questions.explanation: `write`, `write memory`, and `copy running-config startup-config` are three equivalent ways to save the running-config as startup-config.

questions.question: What's the purpose of a rollover cable on a Cisco console port?
options: Connects the router to an ISP over a serial WAN link
Connects a laptop to the RJ45 console port; pins 1-8, 2-7, 3-6, 4-5
Connects two switches for a redundant uplink
Provides power over Ethernet to the console
questions.explanation: A rollover cable connects to the RJ45 console port with pinout 1-8, 2-7, 3-6, 4-5 — typically paired with a DB9/USB adapter for modern laptops.

questions.question: What are the default console settings on a Cisco device?
options: 115200 bps, 8 data bits, 1 stop bit, no parity, no flow control
9600 bps, 8 data bits, 1 stop bit, no parity, no flow control
9600 bps, 7 data bits, 2 stop bits, even parity, hardware flow control
38400 bps, 8 data bits, 1 stop bit, no parity, software flow control
questions.explanation: Default Cisco console settings: 9600 baud, 8 data bits, 1 stop bit, no parity, no flow control.

questions.question: Which command lets you run a privileged-EXEC show command from inside a config mode?
questions.explanation: The `do` prefix runs a privileged EXEC command (like `show running-config`) from inside any config mode.

questions.question: After 3 incorrect enable-password attempts, what CLI message does IOS display?
questions.explanation: IOS responds with `Bad secrets` after three incorrect password attempts at the enable prompt.

questions.question: Are IOS passwords case-sensitive?
options: Only when type-7 encryption is enabled
questions.explanation: IOS passwords are case-sensitive — `CCNA` and `ccna` are treated as different passwords.

questions.question: What difference exists between `running-config` and `startup-config`?
options: `running-config` is the active config edited live; `startup-config` is loaded on reboot
`startup-config` runs first, then swaps to `running-config` after 60 seconds
They are identical copies kept for redundancy
questions.explanation: The running-config is the current active configuration edited as commands are entered; startup-config is the saved copy loaded at boot.
